content.text: ChromaDB and FAISS are the two most commonly used lightweight vector tools in the LLM application stack.  They sit at opposite ends of the abstraction spectrum:
is a developer-friendly vector database that manages collections, embeddings, metadata, and persistence in a single package;

(Facebook AI Similarity Search) is a high-performance C++/Python library that gives you raw index building and querying but leaves persistence, ID mapping, and metadata entirely to your code.  Understanding both tools -- and when to reach for each -- is fundamental for building RAG pipelines at any scale.

attrs.alt: Chroma vs FAISS architecture: Chroma (left) shows ephemeral/persistent client modes, Collection with ids/documents/embeddings/metadatas fields, query flow, and server mode; FAISS (right) shows index types (FlatL2, HNSW, IVF, IVF_PQ), manual ID mapping, and manual persistence via write_index/read_index

content.text: Chroma is an open-source vector database built specifically for LLM workflows.  Its API is intentionally minimal: you create a
, add documents (text strings), and query by text.  Chroma handles embedding automatically using a configurable
via sentence-transformers; easily swapped for OpenAI, Cohere, or a custom model).
A Collection stores four parallel arrays per record:
(filterable dicts).  This flat structure makes Chroma extremely easy to use for prototyping without requiring schema definition or index configuration.
chromadb.Client() -- data lost when process exits. Ideal for unit tests and demos.
chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chroma_db') -- data saved to SQLite + parquet segments.  Single-process, suitable for small-scale production.
chromadb.HttpClient() targeting a running chromadb server.  Enables multi-process or multi-host access.  Server started with chromadb run.

import chromadb

# Persistent client -- data saved to './chroma_db' directory
client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chroma_db')

# Create or get a collection (idempotent)
collection = client.get_or_create_collection(
    name='docs',
    # Default embedding: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (sentence-transformers, ~80 MB download)
)

# Add documents -- Chroma auto-embeds the 'documents' strings
collection.add(
    ids=['doc-1', 'doc-2', 'doc-3'],
    documents=[
        'HNSW is a graph-based ANN algorithm for similarity search.',
        'Product quantization compresses vectors into compact codes.',
        'Pinecone is a fully managed vector database service.',
    ],
    metadatas=[
        {'source': 'ml-blog', 'chapter': 3},
        {'source': 'ml-blog', 'chapter': 4},
        {'source': 'pinecone-docs', 'chapter': 1},
    ],
)

print(collection.count())  # 3
Chroma assigns the embedding automatically by passing the
strings through the EmbeddingFunction.  If you have pre-computed embeddings, pass them directly via the
(or include both -- Chroma stores whichever you provide).

# Text query -- Chroma embeds the query string and runs ANN search
results = collection.query(
    query_texts=['approximate nearest neighbour graph'],
    n_results=2,
    include=['documents', 'distances', 'metadatas'],
)

print(results['ids'])        # [['doc-1', 'doc-2']]
print(results['distances'])  # [[0.182, 0.431]]
print(results['documents'])  # [['HNSW is...', 'Product quantization...']]

# Metadata filter -- only return docs from 'ml-blog'
results = collection.query(
    query_texts=['vector compression'],
    n_results=5,
    where={'source': {'$eq': 'ml-blog'}},
    include=['documents', 'metadatas', 'distances'],
)

# Numeric filter
results = collection.query(
    query_texts=['similarity search'],
    n_results=3,
    where={'chapter': {'$gte': 3}},
)

# Combined filter
results = collection.query(
    query_texts=['embeddings'],
    n_results=5,
    where={'$and': [
        {'source': {'$eq': 'ml-blog'}},
        {'chapter': {'$gt': 2}},
    ]},
)
Chroma uses a MongoDB-compatible filter syntax (
) applied as a post-filter after ANN retrieval.  For very selective filters, request a larger
for pure metadata lookup without a vector query.
The default embedding model (MiniLM) runs locally via sentence-transformers.  For production quality, swap it for an OpenAI or Cohere model:

import chromadb
from chromadb.utils import embedding_functions

# OpenAI embedding function
openai_ef = embedding_functions.OpenAIEmbeddingFunction(
    api_key='YOUR_OPENAI_API_KEY',
    model_name='text-embedding-3-small',
)

client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chroma_db')
collection = client.get_or_create_collection(
    name='docs_openai',
    embedding_function=openai_ef,
)

collection.add(
    ids=['a', 'b'],
    documents=['HNSW graph search', 'Product quantization compression'],
)

# Query automatically uses the same OpenAI model
results = collection.query(query_texts=['ANN algorithms'], n_results=2)

The embedding function used at query time must match the one used at insert time.  Chroma stores the embedding function name in the collection metadata and raises an error if you try to query a collection with a different function.  This prevents silent recall degradation from model mismatch.
For multi-process or multi-host access, run Chroma as a standalone server:

# Start the Chroma server (persists to ./chroma_data)
pip install 'chromadb[server]'
chroma run --path ./chroma_data --port 8000

import chromadb

# Remote HTTP client -- same API as embedded client
client = chromadb.HttpClient(host='localhost', port=8000)

# All collection operations work identically
collection = client.get_or_create_collection('docs')
collection.add(ids=['x'], documents=['Vector search at scale'])
results = collection.query(query_texts=['ANN'], n_results=1)
FAISS is a C++ library (with Python bindings) from Meta AI Research for efficient dense vector search.  It is

a database: it provides no persistence, no metadata storage, no server, and no string IDs.  FAISS assigns sequential integer IDs (0, 1, 2, ...) to vectors and returns those integers -- your application code maps them back to document IDs and fetches metadata from an external store.

FAISS is the right tool when you need maximum performance, GPU acceleration, or fine-grained control over the ANN algorithm and are willing to manage persistence and metadata yourself.  It is also the engine embedded inside many higher-level systems: Weaviate, pgvector, and LangChain's FAISS wrapper all use FAISS indexes under the hood.
Installing and Building a FAISS Index

# CPU-only (most common)
pip install faiss-cpu

# GPU (requires CUDA, significantly faster for large indexes)
pip install faiss-gpu

import faiss
import numpy as np

# Simulate a corpus of 10,000 vectors, d=128 dimensions
d = 128
N = 10_000
corpus = np.random.randn(N, d).astype('float32')  # FAISS requires float32

# ── Option 1: Exact brute-force (small collections)
flat_index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
flat_index.add(corpus)
print(flat_index.ntotal)  # 10000

# ── Option 2: HNSW (best recall/latency, in-memory)
hnsw_index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(d, 32)   # M=32
hnsw_index.hnsw.efConstruction = 200
hnsw_index.add(corpus)

# ── Option 3: IVF (requires training)
quantiser = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
ivf_index = faiss.IndexIVFFlat(quantiser, d, 100)  # nlist=100
ivf_index.train(corpus)   # train on a representative sample
ivf_index.add(corpus)

# Search (same API for all index types)
query = np.random.randn(1, d).astype('float32')
hnsw_index.hnsw.efSearch = 64  # query-time beam width
distances, indices = hnsw_index.search(query, k=5)
print('Top-5 indices:', indices[0])    # e.g. [4821, 1203, 9401, 7, 88]
print('Distances:    ', distances[0])  # L2 distances
ID Mapping -- Connecting FAISS to Your Data
FAISS IDs are sequential integers assigned in insertion order.  To use FAISS in a real application, you need to map FAISS integer IDs back to meaningful document identifiers.  Two patterns:

import faiss, numpy as np, json

documents = [
    {'id': 'doc-a', 'text': 'HNSW builds a layered proximity graph.'},
    {'id': 'doc-b', 'text': 'IVF clusters the corpus into cells.'},
    {'id': 'doc-c', 'text': 'PQ compresses vectors with codebooks.'},
]
# Maintain a list that mirrors insertion order
id_map = [doc['id'] for doc in documents]   # ['doc-a', 'doc-b', 'doc-c']

d = 128
index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(d, 32)
# Pre-compute embeddings (your embedding model here)
embeddings = np.random.randn(len(documents), d).astype('float32')  # placeholder
index.add(embeddings)

# Query and map back to document IDs
query_vec = np.random.randn(1, d).astype('float32')
_, faiss_ids = index.search(query_vec, k=2)
matched_ids = [id_map[i] for i in faiss_ids[0] if i >= 0]  # i=-1 means no result
print(matched_ids)  # e.g. ['doc-a', 'doc-c']
Pattern 2 -- IndexIDMap for arbitrary integer IDs

import faiss, numpy as np

# Wrap any index with IndexIDMap to use your own 64-bit integer IDs
base_index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(128)
index = faiss.IndexIDMap(base_index)

# Use your own IDs (e.g. database primary keys)
my_ids = np.array([1001, 1002, 1003], dtype='int64')
vectors = np.random.randn(3, 128).astype('float32')
index.add_with_ids(vectors, my_ids)

_, ids = index.search(np.random.randn(1, 128).astype('float32'), k=2)
print(ids[0])  # e.g. [1001, 1003]  -- your IDs, not FAISS offsets
Persistence -- Saving and Loading FAISS Indexes

import faiss, json

# Save the FAISS index to disk
faiss.write_index(hnsw_index, 'my_index.faiss')

# Save the ID map and metadata separately
with open('id_map.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(id_map, f)

# --- Later / different process ---

# Load the index
loaded_index = faiss.read_index('my_index.faiss')
loaded_index.hnsw.efSearch = 64  # query-time params must be reset after loading

# Load the ID map
with open('id_map.json') as f:
    loaded_id_map = json.load(f)

# Ready to search
_, faiss_ids = loaded_index.search(query_vec, k=5)
results = [loaded_id_map[i] for i in faiss_ids[0] if i >= 0]
FAISS index files are portable across machines with the same architecture.  The HNSW efSearch parameter is not persisted -- it defaults to 16 after loading, which gives poor recall.
reset query-time parameters (efSearch, nprobe) after loading an index.
Low -- raw index, you manage everything
LLM prototyping, RAG dev, small prod
Both Chroma and FAISS are first-class vector stores in LangChain, allowing you to use them interchangeably within the same retrieval chain:

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma, FAISS
from langchain_core.documents import Document

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model='text-embedding-3-small')

docs = [
    Document(page_content='HNSW builds a layered graph.', metadata={'source': 'a'}),
    Document(page_content='IVF clusters into cells.', metadata={'source': 'b'}),
]

# Chroma vector store
chroma_vs = Chroma.from_documents(
    documents=docs,
    embedding=embeddings,
    persist_directory='./chroma_langchain',
)

# FAISS vector store (wraps faiss-cpu with metadata dict)
faiss_vs = FAISS.from_documents(docs, embeddings)
faiss_vs.save_local('./faiss_langchain')     # saves index + docstore
faiss_vs = FAISS.load_local('./faiss_langchain', embeddings,
                             allow_dangerous_deserialization=True)

# Both implement the same VectorStore interface
for vs in [chroma_vs, faiss_vs]:
    hits = vs.similarity_search('ANN graph traversal', k=2)
    for h in hits:
        print(h.page_content[:50])
Forgetting to reset FAISS efSearch after loading.
faiss.read_index() resets efSearch to 16.  Always set index.hnsw.efSearch = 64 (or higher) immediately after loading.
If you add documents with OpenAI embeddings but query with MiniLM, results will be meaningless.  Chroma validates the function name but cannot detect silent model version mismatches.
FAISS has no persistence by default, no metadata, and no string IDs.  Wrap it in a higher-level class or use LangChain's FAISS VectorStore if you need metadata and persistence.
Training FAISS IVF on the query set.
IVF must be trained on a representative sample of the corpus, not the queries.  Training on the query distribution creates misclustered cells and degrades recall.
Using Chroma beyond its scale limits.
Chroma is not designed for more than a few million vectors in persistent mode.  For larger corpora, migrate to Milvus, Weaviate, or Pinecone.
Use Chroma for prototyping and collections under ~1M vectors.  It has the lowest setup cost and best developer experience for RAG pipelines.
Use FAISS when you need GPU-accelerated search, a specific index type not supported by higher-level databases, or when embedding inside a library that uses FAISS natively.
Always specify the embedding function explicitly when creating a Chroma collection -- don't rely on the default MiniLM for production quality.
After loading a FAISS index from disk, immediately set efSearch (HNSW) or nprobe (IVF) to your production values.
For LangChain-based RAG, start with Chroma (simpler, persistent, no extra infra) and switch to Milvus or Pinecone if you outgrow single-node capacity.
In FAISS, always check for index values of -1 in search results -- these indicate fewer results than requested (empty regions in the index).

Chroma is a batteries-included vector store with automatic embedding, built-in metadata storage and filtering, and three persistence modes.  It is the fastest path from text to vector search for LLM applications.
FAISS is a high-performance similarity-search library with no built-in persistence, metadata, or server.  It returns integer IDs that you map to your own document store.
Chroma is best for prototyping and small-to-medium production collections (up to ~1M vectors).  FAISS is best for performance-critical or GPU-accelerated pipelines.
Both integrate with LangChain via the VectorStore interface and can be swapped without changing retrieval chain code.
For million-plus vector workloads, prefer Milvus, Weaviate, or Pinecone.  Chroma and FAISS are single-node tools.
The critical FAISS pitfall: reset efSearch/nprobe after loading a saved index -- these query-time parameters are not persisted and default to low values.
